era_context: The mid-6th century BCE spans the late Archaic period in Greece, the height of the Neo-Babylonian Empire under Nebuchadnezzar II, the early reign of the Achaemenid Persian Empire under Cyrus II, and the lifetime of Siddhartha Gautama (the Buddha) in northern India. This is an era of philosophical ferment, imperial consolidation, and the emergence of axial-age thinkers across multiple civilisations.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources; archaeological record incomplete)
Northern Europe beyond Greek and Roman awareness (sparse contemporary documentation)
Mesoamerica (no written records contemporary with this year; Maya civilisation in Classic period development)

events.title: Birth of Siddhartha Gautama (traditional date)
events.description: According to Buddhist tradition, Siddhartha Gautama, who would become known as the Buddha, was born in Lumbini in the Shakya kingdom. This date is derived from later Buddhist chronologies and is highly uncertain; modern scholarship places his birth anywhere from 623 to 480 BCE. The event is foundational to Buddhism and profoundly shaped Asian intellectual and spiritual history, though the historical Siddhartha's early life is largely inaccessible to modern verification.

sources.name: Buddhist Pali Canon (Jataka tales, Mahavagga); Sanskrit texts (Lalitavistara Sutra)
Ashoka's edicts (3rd century BCE) — mention of Buddha but no birth date

events.certainty_note: The traditional date of 564 BCE derives from Theravada Buddhist chronology (Mahayana traditions differ). No contemporary sources exist. Modern scholarship using textual analysis and comparative chronology suggests birth between 623–480 BCE, with increasing scholarly favour for dates closer to 480 BCE. The event is historically significant as a religious founding, but the precise year is not historically verifiable.

events.title: Cyrus II consolidates Persian control over Lydia and western Anatolia
events.description: Following the fall of Lydia (traditionally dated 546 BCE, but consolidation continued through the 540s), Cyrus II of Persia secured Persian hegemony over the former Lydian territories and Greek Ionian cities. This period saw the integration of wealthy Anatolian and Levantine territories into the Persian administrative system, establishing the foundation of the Achaemenid Empire's western frontier and setting the stage for later Greco-Persian conflicts.
key_figures: Cyrus II (Cyrus the Great, Achaemenid king)
Croesus (former Lydian king, now under Persian suzerainty)
events.certainty_note: The fall of Lydia is traditionally dated to 546 BCE by Herodotus; consolidation of Persian control extended over several years. The Nabonidus Chronicle and Cyrus Cylinder provide near-contemporary Babylonian and Persian perspectives. Dating to 564 BCE is approximate; the process likely spanned 546–540 BCE.

events.title: Nebuchadnezzar II's reign continues; Babylon at height of power
events.description: Nebuchadnezzar II (r. 605–562 BCE) was in the final years of his reign, during which Babylon reached its zenith as a military and cultural power. The city's walls, gates (including the Ishtar Gate), and the Tower of Babel were completed or maintained during this period. Babylonian astronomy, mathematics, and literature flourished. However, Nebuchadnezzar's death in 562 BCE would trigger succession disputes that weakened the empire.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Babylon (excavations by Koldewey, early 20th century)

events.certainty_note: Nebuchadnezzar II's reign dates are well-established from Babylonian chronicles and king lists. His death in 562 BCE is confirmed by multiple sources. The year 564 BCE falls within his documented reign.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional Buddhist date of 564 BCE for the Buddha's birth is disputed by modern scholarship. Theravada sources place it at 544 BCE; Mahayana sources vary widely. Recent scholarly consensus, based on textual analysis and comparison with Ashoka's reign (which can be more precisely dated), increasingly favours a birth date closer to 480 BCE or even later. The significance of the 564 BCE date is primarily religious and chronological rather than historically verifiable. Additionally, the 'fall of Lydia' in 546 BCE, while traditionally attributed to Cyrus II, may have involved a more gradual process of Persian expansion and Lydian decline rather than a single military conquest.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 564 BCE is uneven. Babylonian sources (cuneiform chronicles) provide reliable contemporary records for the Neo-Babylonian Empire. Greek sources (Herodotus) are valuable but written a century later and reflect Greek perspectives. Buddhist texts are much later compilations with legendary elements. Archaeological evidence from Babylon is rich but does not provide year-by-year precision. The record is heavily weighted toward the Near East and Mediterranean; Indian sources are sparse and late. Sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, and the Americas have no contemporary written documentation for this year.

graph_edges.note: Cyrus II's consolidation of Anatolia set the stage for later Persian-Greek conflicts
Nebuchadnezzar II's death in 562 BCE triggered succession crisis in Babylon
